title: What an American thinks about Bulgaria

transcript.text: Hello, this video is what I think about Bulgaria, what an American thinks about Bulgaria.
Bulgaria is very friendly. The people there are very friendly.
Very bubbly and laughy. They laugh a lot.
Hee hee hee, hee hee, just like the Macedonians.
The Macedonians are some of my favorite people too. They laugh a lot.
And I heard the Macedonians and the Bulgarians are very similar, like genetically.
Or, you know, ethnically. Their language is very similar and everything, but they're in separate countries.
But their personalities are very similar as well. They're very bubbly, cheery, happy people.
I was in Macedonia and I stayed, the couch surfing, I stayed with these people and they were really friendly.
They made food and shared it with me and the man and the woman, they were really friendly men.
Yeah, super friendly.
Sofia is their capital city. Their capital city was very, very, it's a pretty capital city.
It's pretty big, but it's not like totally big.
I don't know, I would guess in terms of American judgmentations of, I don't know, a couple hundred thousand or something.
But they have this really big park right next to the central town there, which is like a labyrinth of trails.
And I saw a lot of condoms out there in the middle of nowhere. I guess that's where they go to do the nasty.
But they had this little kiddie jungle gym place, you know, in the middle of the woods there where they do the obstacle course.
They're connected with the rope and they have to climb through the obstacle course and stuff.
But, yeah, but the main central city there, the main area, it's pretty.
They have the walking area, the pedestrian area where the cars can go through but only the buses.
And, yeah, surrounded by some mountains in the south, they have some nice mountains.
The southern part of the country is very hilly.
It's not flat. It's like you're either going straight up or straight down, but not on big hills.
It's just like, whoa, whoa, up, down, so it's difficult.
And there were some small towns, you know, but not, they didn't look totally ancient.
Like, say, in Spain or Portugal where they were all beyond walls.
They just looked like normal towns without the walls.
They didn't look totally ancient.
Sofia has a nice-looking mosque there, old mosque.
But a lot of the town there before was, on the western side, was very bumpy.
Lots of cobbles, lots of cobblestones.
So, it's an old city.
And they haven't gotten around to paving over those cobblestones.
In the middle of the city, I don't, maybe they don't want to.
And they have the tram on there, on the rails, which you don't see very often anymore.
Or, well, I guess you see it some.
They have those in Denver.
But, yeah, and then I took the train away from there, so.
So, yeah.
Bulgarians seem quite friendly.
Okay, that's what an American thinks about Bulgaria.
Thank you.
Thanks, man.
Alright.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
